serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of lemongrass chicken rice as served in a Singaporean hawker centre or Vietnamese eatery typically weighs approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 180 g of steamed white rice and 120 g of braised lemongrass chicken with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass Chicken Rice (Com Ga Kho Sa Ot) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lemongrass Chicken Rice (Com Ga Kho Sa Ot), cr_id G00010533, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Com Ga Kho Sa Ot. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked white jasmine/long-grain rice, which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 15.12 × 3.0) / 100 ≈ 16.4 for a 300 g serving (45.36 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg per 100 g, partially offset by potassium 182 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce during preparation, and avoid adding table condiments, as a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 972 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 15.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion significantly or substitute with brown rice to lower the glycaemic load, as a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 45 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate that drives post-meal blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg per 100 g and cholesterol 13 mg per 100 g; saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.0 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, prioritising reduced-sodium preparation and smaller rice portions, as the sodium content and moderate fat profile require careful portion control to remain within heart-healthy dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory lemongrass (citral) and capsaicin from chilli; low saturated fat; nightshade content from chilli
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to anti-inflammatory compounds in lemongrass and chilli, but individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor joint response to chilli and limit white rice portions to reduce inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemongrass Chicken Rice, known in Vietnamese as Cơm Gà Kho Sả Ớt (romanized: Com Ga Kho Sa Ot), is a Vietnamese-origin rice dish widely available in Singapore's hawker centres and Vietnamese eateries. The dish consists of steamed white jasmine rice served alongside chicken pieces that have been braised or dry-cooked (kho) in a fragrant sauce of lemongrass (sả), fresh chilli (ớt), fish sauce, garlic, shallots, sugar, and black pepper. The braising technique (kho) produces a caramelised, intensely aromatic sauce that coats the chicken with a balance of savoury, mildly sweet, and spicy flavours. The lemongrass imparts a citrusy, floral aroma, while the chilli adds heat and capsaicin-driven warmth. Texturally, the chicken is tender and slightly sticky from the reduced sauce, contrasting with the soft, fluffy white rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate with 1.0 g dietary fibre and 324 mg sodium. The high sodium content from fish sauce is a key clinical consideration for hypertension management. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for type 2 diabetes. Lemongrass contains citral and other bioactive compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free, making it accessible across multiple dietary communities in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a recorded confidence of 0.95 for an exact named dish entry (cr_id G00010533). All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the well-established white rice GI anchor from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese-Singaporean braised chicken rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010533 as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 1.0 g per 100 g based on typical chicken-and-rice dish composition from HPB comparable entries; this value is an approximation and should be updated when HPB publishes a direct saturated fat figure. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for Com Ga Kho Sa Ot exists in approved sources. The dish is classified as Vietnamese-Singaporean reflecting its Vietnamese culinary origin and widespread presence in Singapore's hawker food landscape. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Vietnamese per Singaporean dish and Vietnamese dish requirements.
